extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a thermal incubator that forms a cyber‑physical system (CPS). It consists of a styrofoam insulated container, a resistive heat‑bed, an internal fan for air mixing, two temperature sensors inside the box, one temperature sensor outside the box, a Raspberry Pi that runs the controller software, a low‑level driver that interfaces with the sensors and actuators, and a RabbitMQ communication server that mediates messages between the driver and the controller. The plant’s goal is to reach and maintain a prescribed temperature inside the box regardless of the contents, using a bang‑bang control strategy with configurable lower‑limit (LL), upper‑limit (UL), heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C). Assumptions for modelling include uniform temperature distribution (ensured by the fan), negligible heat storage in the walls, constant air specific heat capacity, and (for Model A) that all supplied electrical power is converted to heat in the air.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides (i) calibration services that automatically adjust the parameters of the two thermal models (Model A and Model B) using the time‑stamped sensor data streamed from the incubator; (ii) monitoring services that continuously compare the measured temperature against the controller limits (LL, UL) and can flag safety or performance violations; (iii) prediction/simulation services that run the calibrated models to reproduce and forecast the incubator’s temperature dynamics under current operating conditions; and (iv) advanced visualisation support as a value‑adding DT capability, enabling users to view the real‑time status of the incubator.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation of the Digital Twin relies on the following tools and enablers: a RabbitMQ message broker serves as the communication server, allowing the controller and the low‑level driver to exchange commands and sensor readings; a low‑level driver abstracts the hardware interface to the temperature sensors and actuators and periodically uploads measurements to RabbitMQ; the controller, deployed on a Raspberry Pi, executes a bang‑bang control algorithm with parameters LL, UL, H and C; the open‑source hardware and software specifications are hosted in a GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example‑incubator); two physics‑based simulation models (Model A and Model B) are implemented to predict internal temperature, with Model B relaxing the assumption that all electrical power is converted to heat; calibration algorithms update model parameters from sensor data; all components are orchestrated through the message‑based architecture.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The low‑level driver periodically reads temperature sensor values and publishes them to a RabbitMQ message broker; the controller also subscribes to the same broker for commands and state updates. Synchronisation between the physical twin and the digital components is thus achieved through asynchronous message passing over RabbitMQ, but the documents do not specify exact sampling frequencies, latency bounds, or temporal granularity.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The described system employs a single Digital Twin instance that centrally aggregates all data and services for the incubator. The architecture is centralized: the RabbitMQ broker acts as the single point of coordination, and all DT modules (visualisation, calibration, anomaly detection, controller adaptation) operate within the same logical DT framework. No hierarchical or multiple twin instances are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The digital-twin constellation consists of a Raspberry Pi‑based controller, a low-level driver, a RabbitMQ message broker, calibration algorithms that update the thermal models, and monitor components that evaluate safety and optimality; coordination is realized through asynchronous message passing via RabbitMQ, with calibration results feeding the models and monitors, and human operators intervening when violations are detected.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: External integration is performed through the RabbitMQ message broker, which serves as the protocol for data exchange between the DT components and any external services. The hardware and software artefacts are released as open‑source code in a public repository, enabling reuse in other projects, but no additional interoperability standards or connections to other enterprise systems are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The incubator DT incorporates two analytical thermal models derived from the heat equation Q = cmΔT. Model A uses two free parameters and assumes that all supplied electrical power is converted to heat in the air (Assumption 4), while Model B relaxes this assumption and uses four free parameters, improving prediction of the heatbed’s thermal inertia. Both models are behavioural/analytical and are calibrated on‑line: the calibration algorithm (section 3.3) adjusts model parameters using temperature measurements. Data originate from three temperature sensors (two internal, one external); the low‑level driver reads the sensor values periodically and publishes them as messages to a RabbitMQ message broker. The controller on the Raspberry Pi also publishes its state (heating/fan commands) to RabbitMQ. DT components subscribe to these streams: a real‑time visualisation service stores the incoming time‑series into a time‑series database and renders dashboards; an on‑demand calibration service consumes the same data to re‑estimate model parameters; a Kalman‑filter based anomaly‑detection/heatbed‑state estimator consumes model B and sensor data; and what‑if simulation modules run co‑simulations using either live or historical data to explore alternative controller settings.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is provided by a heating element (heat‑bed) and a fan inside the insulated box. Both actuators are controlled by a Raspberry‑Pi based bang‑bang controller that issues binary ON/OFF commands for heating duration H and waiting duration C, bounded by lower (LL) and upper (UL) temperature limits. Commands are sent via the RabbitMQ communication server and the low‑level driver translates them to hardware signals. The low‑level driver embeds a safety protocol that forces the actuators into a safe state on error. Actuation range is limited to on/off states; the controller parameters (LL, UL, H, C) define the permissible temperature band and timing.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Temperature is measured by three digital temperature sensors: two placed inside the incubator box and one mounted outside to monitor ambient temperature. The low‑level driver periodically reads the sensor values (sampling interval not specified) and publishes the measurements to the RabbitMQ broker, where they are stored in a time‑series database for visualization. No explicit accuracy, precision, or sampling frequency is given in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity rests on four explicit assumptions: (1) uniform temperature distribution inside the box (ensured by the internal fan); (2) negligible heat accumulation in the box walls; (3) constant specific heat capacity of the air; (4) all electrical power is transferred to heat the air (relaxed in Model B). Model A adheres to all assumptions, while Model B omits Assumption 4 to better capture the heatbed's thermal inertia. Validation is performed by comparing model predictions with experimentally recorded temperature trajectories; Figure 13 demonstrates that Model B provides a closer match to observed system magnitude and time scale. Calibration (parameter estimation) is carried out automatically using a non‑linear optimisation routine that fits the model to the sensor data, thereby quantifying and reducing model error.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data are acquired by three sensors placed inside the insulated styrofoam box (two for averaging the internal air temperature, one measuring the hottest spot) and one sensor outside the box measuring room temperature. The low‑level driver on the Raspberry Pi periodically reads the raw temperature values, applies a safe‑protocol preprocessing step that averages the two internal sensor readings to obtain the box’s average temperature, and publishes each measurement as a RabbitMQ (AMQP) message to the central RabbitMQ server. The messages contain a timestamp and the temperature value in degrees Celsius. The publication is event‑driven on each sampling interval (frequency not specified in the documents). The DT component subscribes to these messages, forwards them to a time‑series database, and uses them for real‑time visualisation and further algorithmic processing.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin generates control commands that are sent as RabbitMQ messages to the controller running on the Raspberry Pi. Command types include setting the lower and upper temperature limits (LL, UL), issuing heating commands (turning the heatbed on for a duration H), and waiting commands (duration C) that implement a bang‑bang control strategy. Each command is encapsulated in an AMQP message format understood by the controller. The controller validates the command against the safe protocol embedded in the low‑level driver, executes the action on the heatbed or fan, and publishes a state‑update message back to the RabbitMQ server as confirmation of execution. Feedback loops are realised by the controller sharing its current control state (e.g., heating on/off, current temperature limits) through the same broker.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All components communicate via a RabbitMQ message broker (AMQP over TCP/IP). The network topology is a star configuration where the RabbitMQ server acts as the central hub; the Raspberry Pi (running the low‑level driver and controller) and any DT components (calibration module, anomaly detector, visualisation dashboard) connect as clients. RabbitMQ provides persistence, delivery acknowledgements and automatic reconnection, contributing to reliability and redundancy. Security mechanisms such as TLS encryption or authentication are not detailed in the provided documents. Bandwidth and latency requirements are not quantified, but the system is described as lightweight and suitable for real‑time interaction.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The implementation is deployed on‑premise: the physical twin runs on a Raspberry Pi, and the RabbitMQ server is hosted locally (or on a reachable on‑premise machine). DT services such as calibration, Kalman‑filter based state estimation, and visualisation run on the same edge hardware or on a connected workstation; data are stored in a time‑series database that can be accessed by a dashboard. The documents do not specify cloud or edge hosting beyond the Raspberry Pi, nor do they give precise computational or storage sizing, scalability strategies, or high‑availability configurations.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The incubator Digital Twin supplies several decision‑support capabilities as outlined in the digital‑twinning goals. Real‑time visualization is achieved by listening to controller and low‑level driver messages, inserting the time‑stamped data into a time‑series database and rendering it on a dashboard. On‑demand calibration is performed automatically by invoking the calibration routine described in section 3.3 (similar to the tracking simulator). Anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation are realised with a Kalman filter that consumes Model B and fuses measured temperature data to estimate the non‑measured heat‑bed temperature. What‑if simulations are carried out via co‑simulations that feed historic or live Physical Twin data, allowing optimisation of controller parameters. An eight‑step self‑adaptation loop closes the loop: (1) detect a change in plant behaviour through Kalman‑filter anomalies, (2) schedule an experiment to gather relevant data, (3) configure the controller for the experiment, (4) collect the experiment data, (5) recalibrate Model B with the new data, (6) re‑configure the Kalman filter with updated parameters, (7) run what‑if simulations to optimise controller behaviour, and (8) re‑configure the controller accordingly.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The incubator DT incorporates safety mechanisms at both the cyber and physical layers. The low‑level driver embeds a safe protocol that continuously monitors temperature readings and enforces a safe state for the physical twin, preventing unsafe actuation. The controller implements a bang‑bang control strategy with explicit temperature limits (Lower Limit LL and Upper Limit UL) and defined heating (H) and waiting (C) durations, ensuring the heat‑bed is not over‑driven. Communication between the controller, low‑level driver and the digital twin is mediated by a RabbitMQ message broker, which provides reliable message delivery for safety‑critical commands. Anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation are performed by a Kalman filter that processes Model B predictions together with real‑time sensor data, enabling early detection of deviations from expected thermal behavior. These combined measures constitute the security and safety considerations described in the documents.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
